Here’s something people in the UK might not have considered: In 1946 they introduced the tv license, which we have to pay if you own a TV irrespective of whether you watch BBC or not.

This funded the BBC who did not rely on advertising for revenue. Later channels like ITV(1955) and Channel 4(1982) had adds. 

So you could argue that subscription based tv has been around for longer than we think.

"Every streaming service is raising prices. How come?

The price of a streaming subscription is going way up because the services were too inexpensive for too long.

Internet companies thrive by undercutting the establishment on price. Netflix, Amazon and Google all offered products — be it TV, books or news — more cheaply than the legacy players. These low prices made their services popular, and some of the companies used that popularity to raise money during a time of low interest rates. They needed that money to fund unprofitable businesses.

i'm a churner. i have a spreadsheet set up with everything i want to watch, the platforms they're on, and their runtime. if one gets high enough, i subscribe for a month, and usually end up watching other things on there that i didn't even have listed, just because i was perusing. it's a bit of a hassle, and it does run into the issue of "ooh, i really want to watch that one movie", but not currently being subscribed anywhere that it's streaming. that being said, i don't really see a solution to that. we don't want one company to own all streaming, and they need income to acquire and create IP. there are two main problems that i see.

one, a lack of growth is seen as failing. if every single person on the planet was subscribed to netflix, their next quarter would have no growth. the stock market really screws things up, where every quarter requires growth despite the fact that there's obvious going to be a point where that plateaus no matter how popular the product is.

two, we are quickly losing one-time-purchase products, at least digitally. for many movies and shows, you have to be subscribed any time you want to watch it. there's no other option. and if they no longer want to stream it? your only option is pirating it. it makes sense for a continued service, something like live streaming or a game that uses the companies servers to run online or updated versions, but everything else? it makes no sense. they made the thing, no new work is going into it. there's no reason to pay more than once, except that they insist on hosting the product. i get it, it's to "stop people from being able to pirate it", but it doesn't even do that.

software has done the same thing. adobe is a prime example. i learned photoshop and illustrator in the late 2000's by buying an edition of them. it was a big purchase, but i learned that version and had it indefinitely. if i wanted a newer version, i could pay to update it. now, you cannot buy them. you have to subscribe, paying for updates even if you don't want them. and you can't even subscribe to an individual product. where there used to be cheaper options that were just a one-time cost for those that didn't need to stay up to date, it's now a minimum cost that is not feasible for anyone that doesn't use the software heavily.

The REAL reason your subscription is increasing is that the DOJ has been working to silently repeal the Paramount Decrees for years. The entertainment industry is losing important anti-trust regulations. We now have a bunch of streaming companies that are vertically integrated, and studios like Disney and Paramount are pushing up Netflix's prices. Netflix has to shell out more in MGs or produce original content to catch up with the libraries of Disney and the like - and even then, it's just an impossible thing to do.

And now that we're moving away from box office numbers, the independent producer is losing their negotiating power. Only the studios know what has been profitable.  Licensing content is hardly an option - they often want to out-right own the content, which not only decreases future profit opportunities but allows the indie producer little opportunity to outright own their libraries. 

I'm shocked none of this was mentioned. There's huge concerns amongst independent producers.

Most comments mention the piracy, which is strongly despised by media companies. But I think the piracy's model of distribution should actually be embraced by those companies, because it dramatically reduces costs.

Streaming services are centralized so they need super expensive servers and thousands of programmers, middle managers and upper managers. But to provide seamless experience they also need CDNs and edge servers, which further raises costs. And when multiple streaming companies exists, each needs its own expensive infrastructure while capturing only some users. So currently we're multiplying costs and dividing income - not very sustainable.

On the other hand, in piracy's distribution model everything is distributed. Storage consists of inexpensive home servers which are also used for other purposes, you CDN is your country's or even city's network. Your edge server is at your own or neighbor's home. So when you and your partner want to watch the same thing but at different times, data doesn't need to be transferred twice, because it can be temporarily stored locally. And there are no CEO's or managers to be payed.

If someone could embrace that model while fairly compensating media creators (and only them, not tens of CEO's alongside) then costs for users will be low and no laws broken.
